note: Named SOURCE passages for AI card generation (Friday AI Feature 2). ORIGINAL statements of standard probability facts, authored for this fork (no copyrighted text). Each generated card must be grounded in, and cite, one of these sources. key_terms are the answer keys the generation rubric checks for (tools/speedrun/evals/generate_eval.py). These are AI INPUTS, so the leakage scan runs them against the held-out item corpus.

sources.name: Bayes theorem note
sources.passage: Bayes' theorem updates a prior probability into a posterior after observing evidence. It states that P(A given B) equals P(B given A) times P(A), divided by P(B). The denominator P(B) is usually expanded with the law of total probability over a partition of outcomes.

sources.name: Conditional probability note
sources.passage: Conditional probability P(A given B) equals P(A and B) divided by P(B), and is defined only when P(B) is positive. It measures the probability of A once B is known to have occurred.

sources.name: Binomial distribution note
sources.passage: A binomial random variable counts the number of successes in n independent trials, each with the same success probability p. Its mean is n times p, and its variance is n times p times one minus p.

sources.name: Exponential distribution note
sources.passage: An exponential random variable models waiting times and is memoryless. With rate lambda, its mean is one over lambda and its variance is one over lambda squared.

sources.name: Expectation note
sources.passage: The expected value of a continuous random variable is the integral of x times its density over the support. Expectation is linear, so E[aX + b] equals a times E[X] plus b.

sources.name: Variance note
sources.passage: Variance measures the spread of a random variable and equals E[X squared] minus the square of E[X]. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance.

sources.name: Covariance note
sources.passage: Covariance measures how two random variables move together and equals E[XY] minus E[X] times E[Y]. Correlation rescales covariance by the product of the standard deviations, so it always lies between minus one and one.

sources.name: Central limit theorem note
sources.passage: The central limit theorem states that the sample mean of many independent, identically distributed variables is approximately normal, with mean mu and variance sigma squared divided by n, for large n.

sources.name: Probability axioms note
sources.passage: A probability is a number between zero and one, the probability of the whole sample space is one, and for disjoint events the probability of their union is the sum of the probabilities. The complement rule says the probability of not A is one minus the probability of A.

sources.name: Counting note
sources.passage: A permutation counts ordered arrangements and equals n factorial divided by n minus k factorial. A combination counts unordered selections and equals n factorial divided by k factorial times n minus k factorial. The multiplication principle multiplies the number of choices across independent stages.

sources.name: Independence note
sources.passage: Two events are independent when the probability of their intersection equals the product of their probabilities, equivalently when conditioning on one does not change the other. For independent components connected in series the system works with the product of the working probabilities.

sources.name: Addition and multiplication rules note
sources.passage: The addition rule says the probability of A or B equals the probability of A plus the probability of B minus the probability of both. The multiplication rule says the probability of both equals the probability of A times the conditional probability of B given A. For mutually exclusive events the intersection is zero.

sources.name: PDF and CDF note
sources.passage: A probability density function is nonnegative and integrates to one over its support. The cumulative distribution function at x is the probability that the variable is at most x, which is the integral of the density up to x, so the density is the derivative of the cumulative distribution function.

sources.name: Insurance payment note
sources.passage: With an ordinary deductible d, an insurance policy pays the loss minus d when the loss exceeds d and pays nothing otherwise, that is the maximum of the loss minus d and zero. A policy limit caps the payment. The expected payment integrates the payment function against the loss density.

sources.name: Joint distribution note
sources.passage: A joint density of two variables is nonnegative and integrates to one over the plane. The probability that the pair falls in a region is the double integral of the joint density over that region. For a discrete pair the joint probability mass function sums to one.

sources.name: Marginal and conditional note
sources.passage: The marginal density of X is obtained by integrating the joint density over the other variable. The conditional density of Y given X equals the joint density divided by the marginal density of X, defined wherever that marginal density is positive.

sources.name: Joint moments note
sources.passage: The expected value of a function of two variables is the double integral of the function times the joint density over the plane. In particular the expectation of the product XY is the double integral of xy times the joint density. Expectation is linear, so the expectation of a sum is the sum of the expectations.

sources.name: Order statistics note
sources.passage: For a sample of n independent identically distributed variables, the maximum has cumulative distribution function equal to the common cumulative distribution raised to the n, and the minimum has cumulative distribution one minus one minus the common cumulative distribution raised to the n. Differentiating gives their densities.

sources.name: Linear combinations note
sources.passage: For a linear combination a times X plus b times Y, the mean is a times the mean of X plus b times the mean of Y. The variance is a squared times the variance of X plus b squared times the variance of Y plus two times a times b times the covariance. When X and Y are independent the covariance term is zero so the variances add.
